passages.title: Organic Farming and Chemical Fertilizers
passages.source_html:
Organic Farming and Chemical Fertilizers 


A 


The world’s population continues to climb. And despite the rise of high-tech agriculture, 800 million people don’t get enough to eat. Clearly, it’s time to rethink the food we eat and where it comes from. Feeding 9 billion people will take more than the same old farming practices, especially if we want to do it without felling rainforests and planting every last scrap of prairie. Finding food for all those people will tax farmers’ – and researchers’ – ingenuity to the limit. Yet already, precious aquifers that provide irrigation water for some of the world’s most productive farmlands are drying up or filling with seawater, and arable land in China is eroding to create vast dust storms that redden sunsets as far away as North America. ‘Agriculture must become the solution to environmental problems in 50 years. If we don’t have systems that make the environment better – not just hold the fort-then we’re in trouble,’ says Kenneth Cassman, an agronomist at the University of Nebraska at Lincoln. That view was echoed in January by the Curry report, a government panel that surveyed the future of farming and food in Britain. 


B 


It’s easy to say agriculture has to do better, but what should this friendly farming of the future look like? Concerned consumers come up short at this point, facing what appears to be an ever-widening ideological divide. In one corner are the techno-optimists who put their faith in genetically modified crops, improved agrochemicals and computer-enhanced machinery; in the other are advocates of organic farming, who reject artificial chemicals and embrace back-to-nature techniques such as composting. Both sides cite plausible science to back their claims to the moral high ground, and both bring enough passion to the debate for many people to come away thinking we’re faced with a stark choice between two mutually incompatible options. 


C 


Not so. If you take off the ideological blinkers and simply ask how the world can produce the food it needs with the least environmental cost, a new middle way opens. The key is sustainability: whatever we do must not destroy the capital of soil and water we need to keep on producing. Like today’s organic farming, the intelligent farming of the future should pay much more attention to the health of its soil and the ecosystem it’s part of. But intelligent farming should also make shrewd and locally appropriate use of chemical fertilisers and pesticides. The most crucial ingredient in this new style of agriculture is not chemicals but information about what’s happening in each field and how to respond. Yet ironically, this key element may be the most neglected today. 


D 


Clearly, organic farming has all the warm, fuzzy sentiment on its side. An approach that eschews synthetic chemicals surely runs no risk of poisoning land and water. And its emphasis on building up natural ecosystems seems to be good for everyone. Perhaps these easy assumptions explain why sales of organic food across Europe are increasing by at least 50 per cent per year. 


E 


Going organic sounds idyllic – but it’s native, too. Organic agriculture has its own suite of environmental costs, which can be worse than those of conventional farming, especially if it were to become the world norm. But more fundamentally, the organic versus-chemical debate focuses on the wrong question. The issue isn’t what you put into a farm, but what you get out of it, both in terms of crop yields and pollutants, and what condition the farm is in when you’re done. 


F 


Take chemical fertilisers, which deliver nitrogen, an essential plant nutrient, to crops along with some phosphorus and potassium. It is a mantra of organic farming that these fertilisers are unwholesome, and plant nutrients must come from natural sources. But in fact, the main environmental damage done by chemical fertilisers as opposed to any other kind is through greenhouse gases-carbon dioxide from the fossil fuels used in their synthesis and nitrogen oxides released by their degradation. Excess nitrogen from chemical fertilisers can pollute groundwater, but so can excess nitrogen from organic manures. 


G 


On the other hand, relying solely on chemical fertilisers to provide soil nutrients without doing other things to build healthy soil is damaging. Organic farmers don’t use chemical fertilisers, so they are very good at building soil fertility by working crop residues and manure into the soil, rotating grain with legumes that fix atmospheric nitrogen, and other techniques. 


H 


This generates vital soil nutrients and also creates a soil that is richer in organic matter, so it retains better and is hospitable to the crop’s roots and creatures such as earthworms that help maintain soil fertility. Such soil also holds water better and therefore make more efficient use of both rainfall and irrigation water. And organic matter ties up CO2 in the soil, helping to offset emissions from burning fossil fuels and reduce global warming. 


I 


Advocates of organic farming like to point out that fields managed in this way can produce yields just as high as fields juiced up with synthetic fertilisers. For example, Bill Liebhardt, research manager at the Rodale Institute in Kutztown, Pennsylvania, recently compiled the results of such comparisons for corn, wheat, soybeans and tomatoes in the US and found that the organic fields averaged between 94 and 100 per cent of the yields of nearby conventional crops. 


J 


But this optimistic picture tells only half the story. Farmers can’t grow such crops every year if they want to maintain or build soil nutrients without synthetic fertilisers. They need to alternate with soil-building crops such as pasture grasses and legumes such as alfalfa. So in the long term, the yield of staple grains such as wheat, rice and corn must go down. This is the biggest cost of organic farming. Vaclav Smil of the University of Manitoba in Winnipeg, Canada, estimates that if farmers worldwide gave up the 80 million tonnes of synthetic fertiliser they now use each year, total grain production would fall by at least half. Either farmers would have to double the amount of land they cultivate – at catastrophic cost to natural habitats – or billions of people would starve. 


K 


That doesn’t mean farmers couldn’t get by with less fertiliser. Technologically advanced farmers in wealthy countries, for instance, can now monitor their yields hectare by hectare, or even more finely, throughout a huge field. They can then target their fertiliser to the parts of the field where it will do the most good, instead of responding to average conditions. This increases yield and decreases fertiliser use. Eventually, farmers may incorporate long-term weather forecasts into their planning as well, so that they can cut back on fertiliser use when the weather is likely to make harvests poor anyway, says Ron Olson, an agronomist with Cargill Fertilizer in Tampa, Florida. 


L 


Organic techniques certainly have their benefits, especially for poor farmers. But strict ‘organic agriculture’, which prohibits certain technologies and allows others, isn’t always better for the environment. Take herbicides, for example. These can leach into waterways and poison both wildlife and people. Just last month, researchers led by Tyrone Hayes at the University of California at Berkeley found that even low concentrations of atrazine, the most commonly used weedkiller in the US, can prevent frog tadpoles from developing properly.

groups.title: Questions 1-13
groups.source_html:
Questions 1-4 


Use the information in the passage to match the people (listed A-D) with opinions or deeds below. Write the appropriate letters A-D in boxes 1-4 on your answer sheet.


A Vaclav Smil 

B Bill Liebhardt 

C Kenneth Cassman 

D Ron Olson 


Question / ParagraphABCDEFG
1

1 Use of chemical fertilizer can be optimised by combining weather information.
✅ 正确答案：D

原文答案句：Eventually, farmers may incorporate long-term weather forecasts into their planning as well, so that they can cut back on fertiliser use when the weather is likely to make harvests poor anyway, says Ron Olson, an agronomist with Cargill Fertilizer in Tampa, Florida.

对应段落：K段

审题：题目问的是谁认为可以通过结合天气信息来优化化肥的使用。

定位关键信息：题目中的关键词是"chemical fertilizer"、"optimised"和"weather information"，这些词可以帮助我们在文章中定位到相关的句子。

解题思路：在K段中，我们可以找到Ron Olson说的话，他提到农民可以结合长期天气预报来规划，从而在天气可能使收成不佳的情况下减少化肥的使用。这正好与题目中提到的"通过结合天气信息来优化化肥的使用"相符。因此，答案为D。
2

2 Organic framing yield is nearly equal to traditional ones.
✅ 正确答案：B

原文答案句：Organic farmers like to point out that fields managed in this way can produce yields just as high as fields juiced up with synthetic fertilisers.  

对应段落：I段  

审题：题目问的是关于有机农业产量与传统农业产量相近的观点。  

定位关键信息：利用题干中的关键词"Organic framing yield"和"nearly equal to traditional ones"在文章中定位。  

解题思路：在I段中找到与有机农业产量相关的句子，原文明确提到有机农业的产量可以与使用合成肥料的传统农业产量相当（"just as high as"），与题干中的"nearly equal to"意思一致，因此对应的人是Bill Liebhardt，即B。
3

3 Better agricultural setting is a significant key to solve environmental tough nut.
✅ 正确答案：C

原文答案句：The key is sustainability: whatever we do must not destroy the capital of soil and water we need to keep on producing.

对应段落：A段

审题：题目问“更好的农业环境是解决环境难题的关键”，即询问谁认为可持续农业是解决环境问题的关键。

定位关键信息：题目中的“better agricultural setting”对应原文中的“sustainability”，即可持续性；题目中的“significant key to solve environmental tough nut”对应原文中的“key is sustainability”。

解题思路：原文A段明确提到“可持续性是关键”，并指出未来农业应关注土壤和生态系统的健康，这与题目中“更好的农业环境是解决环境难题的关键”完全一致。因此，答案为C。
4

4 Substantial production loss would happen in case all farmers shifted from using synthetic fertiliser.
✅ 正确答案：A

原文答案句：This is the biggest cost of organic farming. Vaclav Smil of the University of Manitoba in Winnipeg, Canada, estimates that if farmers worldwide gave up the 80 million tonnes of synthetic fertiliser they now use each year, total grain production would fall by at least half.  

对应段落：J段  

审题：题目问的是“如果所有农民都停止使用合成肥料，将发生大规模生产损失”，需要找到关于停止使用合成肥料导致生产损失的观点或事实。  

定位关键信息：通过题干中的关键词“Substantial production loss”（大规模生产损失）、“shifted from using synthetic fertiliser”（停止使用合成肥料）可以定位到J段中关于Vaclav Smil的观点，他提到如果农民停止使用合成肥料，粮食产量将至少下降一半。  

解题思路：J段明确提到Vaclav Smil的研究结果，即如果全球农民放弃使用合成肥料，粮食总产量将至少下降一半，这与题目描述的“大规模生产损失”完全一致。因此，Vaclav Smil是提出这一观点的人，对应选项A。

Questions 5-9 


Do the following statements agree with the information given in the Reading Passage 1? In boxes 5-9 on your answer sheet, write YES if the statement agrees with the information NO if the statement contradicts the information NOT GIVEN if there is no information on this


5

Increasing population, draining irrigation,eroding farmland push agricultural industry to extremity.

正确答案： A. YES

原文答案句：The world’s population continues to climb. And despite the rise of high-tech agriculture, 800 million people don’t get enough to eat. Clearly, it’s time to rethink the food we eat and where it comes from. Feeding 9 billion people will take more than the same old farming practices, especially if we want to do it without felling rainforests and planting every last scrap of prairie. Finding food for all those people will tax farmers’ – and researchers’ – ingenuity to the limit. Yet already, precious aquifers that provide irrigation water for some of the world’s most productive farmlands are drying up or filling with seawater, and arable land in China is eroding to create vast dust storms that redden sunsets as far away as North America.

对应段落：A段

审题：题目问的是“人口增长、灌溉枯竭、耕地侵蚀是否将农业行业推向极端”。

定位关键信息：利用题目的“increasing population”、“draining irrigation”、“eroding farmland”这些关键词在文章中定位答案。

解题思路：原文A段明确提到人口持续增长，需要重新思考食物来源，并且提到了灌溉水源枯竭和耕地侵蚀等问题，这些都表明农业行业面临极端压力，因此题目与原文信息一致。


6

There are only two options for farmers; they use chemical fertiliser or natural approach.

正确答案： B. NO

原文答案句：In one corner are the techno-optimists who put their faith in genetically modified crops, improved agrochemicals and computer-enhanced machinery; in the other are advocates of organic farming, who reject artificial chemicals and embrace back-to-nature techniques such as composting. Both sides cite plausible science to back their claims to the moral high ground, and both bring enough passion to the debate for many people to come away thinking we’re faced with a stark choice between two mutually incompatible options. (B段)

对应段落：B段

审题：题目问的是农民是否只有两种选择：使用化学肥料或自然方法。

定位关键信息：题目中的"chemical fertiliser"和"natural approach"与B段中的"artificial chemicals"和"back-to-nature techniques"对应。

解题思路：B段提到确实存在两种对立的观点，但紧接着在C段开头就指出"Not so."，表明这两种观点并非唯一选择，后面提出了第三种可持续的农业方式。因此，题目说法与原文信息不符，答案为NO。


7

Chemical fertilizer currently are more expensive than the natural fertilisers.

正确答案： C. NOT GIVEN

原文答案句：Not so. If you take off the ideological blinkers and simply ask how the world can produce the food it needs with the least environmental cost, a new middle way opens. The key is sustainability: whatever we do must not destroy the capital of soil and water we need to keep on producing. Like today’s organic farming, the intelligent farming of the future should pay much more attention to the health of its soil and the ecosystem it’s part of. But intelligent farming should also make shrewd and locally appropriate use of chemical fertilisers and pesticides. The most crucial ingredient in this new style of agriculture is not chemicals but information about what’s happening in each field and how to respond. Yet ironically, this key element may be the most neglected today.

对应段落：C段

审题：题目问“化学肥料目前比天然肥料更昂贵”是否与文章信息一致。

定位关键信息：题目中的关键词是“Chemical fertilizer”和“natural fertilisers”，通过定位“chemical fertilisers”可以找到C段提到“chemical fertilisers and pesticides”，但全文并未提及化学肥料与天然肥料的价格比较。

解题思路：文章在C段提到了化学肥料的使用，但并未涉及价格比较，也没有提到天然肥料的价格或成本。因此，题目中的价格比较信息在文章中没有明确说明，无法判断其是否正确。


8

In order to keep nutrient in the soil, organic farmers need to rotate planting method.

正确答案： A. YES

原文答案句：Organic farmers don’t use chemical fertilisers, so they are very good at building soil fertility by working crop residues and manure into the soil, rotating grain with legumes that fix atmospheric nitrogen, and other techniques.

对应段落：G段

审题：题目问“为了保持土壤中的养分，有机农民需要轮换种植方法”是否与文章信息一致。

定位关键信息：利用题目中的关键词“keep nutrient in the soil”和“rotate planting method”定位到G段，该段提到有机农民通过轮换谷物和豆类来增加土壤肥力。

解题思路：原文明确指出有机农民通过轮换谷物和豆类（rotating grain with legumes）来保持土壤肥力，这与题目中的“rotate planting method”一致，因此答案为YES。


9

‘Organic agriculture’ is the way that environment-damaging technologies are all strictly forbidden.

正确答案： B. NO

原文答案句： But strict ‘organic agriculture’, which prohibits certain technologies and allows others, isn’t always better for the environment.

对应段落：L段  

审题：题目问“有机农业”是否是严格禁止所有环境破坏技术的途径。  

定位关键信息：利用题干中的关键词“Organic agriculture”和“environment-damaging technologies”定位到L段，该段提到有机农业prohibit certain technologies。  

解题思路：原文明确指出有机农业会禁止部分环境破坏技术，并不是严格禁止所有环境破坏技术。因此题干的说法与原文信息不符，答案为NO。


Questions 10-13 


Complete the summary below. Choose NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS from the passage for each answer. Write your answers in boxes 10-13 on your answer sheet.


10

Several 10 approaches need to be applied in order that global population wouldn’t go starved. 

正确答案： 10 farming
Question 10

原文答案句：

The world’s population continues to climb. And despite the rise of high-tech agriculture, 800 million people don’t get enough to eat. Clearly, it’s time to rethink the food we eat and where it comes from. Feeding 9 billion people will take more than the same old farming practices, especially if we want to do it without felling rainforests and planting every last scrap of prairie.

对应段落：A段。

审题：题目问的是为了防止全球人口挨饿，需要应用哪些方法。

定位关键信息：题干中的"global population wouldn’t go starved"和"approaches"是关键信息，需要在文章中找到与全球人口挨饿和解决方案相关的内容。

解题思路：根A段提到的"800 million people don’t get enough to eat"和"take more than the same old farming practices"，可以得出需要应用多种农业方法来满足全球人口的粮食需求。因此，答案为"farming"。


11

A team called 11 repeated the viewpoint of a scholar by a survey in British farming. 

正确答案： 11 Curry
Question 11

原文答案句：That view was echoed in January by the Curry report, a government panel that surveyed the future of farming and food in Britain. 

对应段落：A段

审题：题目要求根据给出的摘要，从文章中选择不超过两个单词来填空，题目要求找到一个团队名称，这个团队通过调查重复了某学者的观点。

定位关键信息：利用题目中的关键词“repeated the viewpoint of a scholar by a survey in British farming”来定位答案在文章中的句子。

解题思路：根据原文答案句，可以直接看出“curry”是填空的答案。这是因为题干明确提到“a team called Curry”，这与原文中“by Curry report”部分相匹配。因此，答案为“curry”。


12

More and more European farmers believe in 12 farming these years. 

正确答案： 12 natural##organic
Question 12

原文答案句：

"Perhaps these easy assumptions explain why sales of organic food across Europe are increasing by at least 50 per cent per year."

对应段落： D段

审题：题目要求找到欧洲农民越来越相信的农业类型。

定位关键信息："European farmers believe in"对应原文的"sales of organic food across Europe are increasing""these years"对应原文的"per year"的增长趋势

解题思路：在D段找到关于欧洲农业趋势的描述：发现有机食品在欧洲销售额年增长50%以上，这种销售增长反映了消费者和农民对有机农业的信任从上下文可以确定是"organic"（有机）农业 正确答案： organic



13

The argument of organic against 13 seems in an inaccurate direction.

正确答案： 13 chemical
Question 13

原文答案句：But more fundamentally, the organic versus-chemical debate focuses on the wrong question. 

对应段落：E段  

审题：题目要求填空，问的是什么东西看起来在错误的方向上流动。  

定位关键信息：根据题干中的"seems in an inaccurate direction"定位E段，找到包含这一短语的句子。  

解题思路：原文明确指出"chemical"看起来在错误的方向上流动，因此答案为"chemical"。题干中的"seems in an inaccurate direction"与原文完全一致，直接对应"chemical"这一名词。
